question: You are building a custom TensorFlow model for predicting house prices. Your preprocessing includes scaling numerical variables and one-hot encoding categorical variables. During training, you apply these transforms in your Python script. However, in production, client requests sometimes send raw, unscaled values, causing incorrect predictions. This is an example of training-serving skew. How should you prevent this skew?
options: Use Cloud Dataflow to preprocess the data in batch before training, and write an equivalent Cloud Function in Node.js to preprocess requests before they hit the endpoint.
Use TensorFlow Transform (tf.Transform) to define the preprocessing pipeline. Export the preprocessing graph and prepend it directly to the serving model, so the endpoint accepts raw data.
Increase the regularization parameters (L1/L2) in the TensorFlow model to make it more robust to scale differences.
Ask clients to implement the scaling equations directly in their client-side application before sending API requests.
explanation: TensorFlow Transform (tf.Transform) solves training-serving skew by exporting the preprocessing steps as a TensorFlow computational graph. This graph is exported together with the model, meaning the model endpoint accepts raw inputs and performs the exact same preprocessing under the hood.

question: You have a streaming pipeline that receives user action events from Cloud Pub/Sub. You need to calculate the click-through rate of ads displayed on a website for the last 10 minutes, updating the calculation every 1 minute. Which windowing strategy in Apache Beam (Cloud Dataflow) should you use?
options: Fixed Windows of 10 minutes duration.
Sliding Windows of 10 minutes duration with a 1 minute slide period.
Session Windows with a gap duration of 1 minute.
Global Windows with custom triggers based on element count.
explanation: Sliding windows are used when you want a window of size N that updates every M minutes. In this case, you want the last 10 minutes of data (window size) updated every 1 minute (slide period), which corresponds perfectly to a Sliding Window.

question: Your team is building multiple fraud detection models that all require the same features (e.g., 'number_of_failed_logins_last_24h'). Currently, different data scientists are calculating these features independently, leading to redundant work and inconsistencies in the data. You want to centralize these features. What GCP service should you use?
options: BigQuery Materialized Views.
Vertex AI Feature Store.
Cloud Bigtable.
Cloud Storage bucket with parquet files.
explanation: Vertex AI Feature Store is specifically designed to solve this problem. It acts as a central repository to organize, store, and serve features. It enables feature sharing and reuse across different models/teams, and serves them both offline (high throughput for training) and online (low latency for predictions).

question: You are training a model on a dataset where one of the features is a user's country. The country feature contains 190 unique values. During prediction serving, you notice that the model receives new country values (e.g. newly added regions) that were not present in the training set, causing the model to throw exceptions or predict incorrectly. Which MLOps component or practice should you implement to detect this issue automatically?
options: Use Vertex AI Model Monitoring to detect training-serving skew on the country feature.
Use TFX StatisticsGen to compute prediction metrics.
Set up an Apache Beam pipeline with Fixed Windows.
Deploy a custom container to handle error logging manually.
explanation: Training-serving skew includes differences in the values of features between training and serving datasets (e.g., a feature value that is present during serving but missing during training). Configuring Vertex AI Model Monitoring on this feature will trigger alerts when a significant skew is detected.

question: Your organization stores raw ML training data in Cloud Storage buckets and processed feature tables in BigQuery across multiple GCP projects. The data governance team wants a unified catalog with automatic metadata discovery and lineage tracking, plus automated daily data quality checks (null rate validation, value range checks) that alert on failures before pipeline runs. Which GCP service should you use?
options: Vertex AI Feature Store, as it centralizes feature metadata.
Cloud Data Catalog, as it provides manual tagging of BigQuery tables.
Dataplex, as it provides unified lake governance, automatic metadata discovery across GCS and BigQuery, and native Data Quality Tasks that run on a schedule.
Cloud Dataflow with custom DoFn functions that perform validation and write results to Cloud Logging.
explanation: Dataplex is Google Cloud's unified data management and governance service. It auto-discovers metadata across GCS and BigQuery (via Lakes/Zones/Assets), provides a searchable Data Catalog with lineage, and supports scheduled Data Quality Tasks (SQL-based rule checks) that publish results to Pub/Sub for alerting — all without writing custom code.

question: You are using Vertex AI AutoML Tabular for fraud detection on a dataset with 0.3% positive (fraud) examples. What configurations help the model handle this extreme class imbalance?
options: Increase the training budget; AutoML compensates for class imbalance automatically with more time.

Configure a positive class weight (upweight the minority class) in the AutoML training settings and set the optimization objective to **AUC-PR** (Area Under Precision-Recall Curve) instead of AUC-ROC. AUC-PR is more sensitive to minority class performance in highly imbalanced datasets.
Use stratified sampling to create a perfectly balanced 50/50 dataset by discarding most non-fraud rows.
Use anomaly detection instead — classification cannot handle ratios below 1%.
explanation: At 0.3% positive rate, AUC-ROC can be misleadingly high because the model can score the large negative class well even if it misses all fraud. AUC-PR focuses on precision and recall for the minority class — more meaningful for imbalanced problems. Upweighting the positive class tells AutoML to penalize false negatives more heavily. In the AutoML API, set `optimization_objective='maximize-au-prc'` and `weight_column` or class weights in the training spec.

question: You are building a Vertex AI training pipeline with TFX. You need to ensure feature transformations (e.g., normalization, vocabulary lookup) are applied identically during training and serving without code duplication. Which TFX component enables this?
options: Write feature engineering in a Python preprocessing_fn and wrap it in a separate Cloud Dataflow job.

Use the **TFX Transform component** (`tft.AnalyzeAndTransformDataset`): it materializes the feature engineering logic as a TensorFlow graph saved in a `transform_fn` artifact. This artifact is embedded in the exported SavedModel via `transform_fn_io` and executed during both training data generation and serving — guaranteeing identical transformations with no code duplication.
Use Feature Store to store pre-computed feature values and reference them in the TFX pipeline.
Use TFX StatisticsGen to compute and cache feature statistics for reuse at serving time.
explanation: TFX Transform solves the training-serving skew problem for feature engineering. `preprocessing_fn` defines transformations in TensorFlow ops. `AnalyzeAndTransformDataset` runs two passes: Analyze (compute global statistics like vocabulary, mean, std) and Transform (apply them). The resulting `transform_fn` TensorFlow graph is exported alongside the model and executed at serving time inside the SavedModel — the same TF graph runs in both contexts, making skew impossible.

question: Before training, you need to automatically detect data anomalies: unexpected new categorical values, null rates above 5%, or feature distribution shifts from historical baselines. Which GCP/TFX toolchain is purpose-built for this?
options: Write a Cloud Run script that computes column statistics and compares them to hard-coded thresholds.

Use **TFX StatisticsGen + SchemaGen + ExampleValidator**: StatisticsGen computes statistics over new data; SchemaGen (run once on baseline) defines expected constraints (null rate bounds, allowed values, value ranges); ExampleValidator compares new statistics to the schema and outputs anomalies as a structured artifact. Runs natively in Vertex AI Pipelines.
Enable BigQuery column-level statistics alerts in Cloud Monitoring.
Use Dataprep to visually inspect data quality before each training run.
explanation: The TFX data validation trio: (1) StatisticsGen computes feature statistics (mean, std, quantiles, top values, missing rate) using Apache Beam — scalable to large datasets; (2) SchemaGen infers or loads a schema (TFDV Schema proto) defining expected feature types, value sets, and bounds; (3) ExampleValidator compares new statistics to the schema and flags anomalies (new values, null spikes, distribution shift vs. baseline). This is the standard GCP MLOps data quality gate.

question: Your team has collected 200,000 unlabeled product images for training a defect detection model. You need human annotators to draw bounding boxes around defects. You want a managed service that supports crowd-sourced annotation with built-in quality control (consensus across multiple labelers) and integrates natively with Vertex AI Datasets. Which service should you use?
options: Upload images to Cloud Storage and use a third-party annotation platform (e.g., Scale AI), then manually import labels into Vertex AI.

Use Vertex AI Data Labeling Service: create a labeling job directly on a Vertex AI Image Dataset, specify BOUNDING_BOX as the annotation type, define the defect label taxonomy, and submit to a pool of human labelers. The service manages labeler assignment, consensus scoring, and delivers completed annotations directly back to the dataset.
Use AutoML Vision active learning — upload unlabeled images and AutoML will label them automatically without human labelers.
Write a Cloud Run service that calls the Cloud Vision API to auto-annotate defects and stores bounding boxes in BigQuery.
explanation: Vertex AI Data Labeling Service is the managed annotation service integrated with Vertex AI Datasets. It supports bounding box, classification, segmentation, and text annotation tasks. Human labelers are sourced from Google's labeling pool or your own crowd. Quality control is built in via multi-labeler consensus. Completed annotations are delivered directly to the Vertex AI Dataset, ready for AutoML or custom training — no data movement required. Cloud Vision API can detect objects generically but cannot be trained to label domain-specific defects with custom taxonomy.

question: You are building an online recommendation system. The model needs a real-time feature 'clicks_in_last_5_minutes' per user, updated continuously as click events arrive. This feature must be readable with under 10ms latency at prediction time. How should you architect the real-time feature pipeline?
options: Use a Cloud Dataflow batch job that runs every 5 minutes, reads all click events from BigQuery, aggregates per user, and writes to a BigQuery table that the prediction endpoint queries at request time.

Stream click events via Cloud Pub/Sub into a Cloud Dataflow pipeline that computes the 5-minute windowed aggregation per user and writes results to Vertex AI Feature Store online store (Bigtable-backed) for sub-10ms feature serving at prediction time.
Use Vertex AI Feature Store's scheduled offline ingestion from Cloud Storage to batch-compute the feature every 5 minutes.
Compute the feature inside the prediction endpoint container at request time by querying the last 5 minutes of click events from Cloud Spanner.
explanation: Real-time feature pipelines on GCP follow this pattern: (1) Events arrive in Cloud Pub/Sub; (2) Cloud Dataflow reads from Pub/Sub and computes windowed aggregations (5-minute windows using Apache Beam); (3) Aggregated features are written to Vertex AI Feature Store's online store, which is backed by Cloud Bigtable for sub-10ms key-value reads; (4) At prediction time, the serving pipeline reads from the Feature Store online store. Batch polling every 5 minutes introduces up to 5-minute staleness and uses higher-latency BigQuery reads. Computing at request time adds prediction latency and unnecessary load on the transactional database.

question: Your ML training dataset is stored in a BigQuery table with 5 billion rows spanning 3 years of transactions. Training jobs query only the most recent 90 days of data. Each query scans the full table, taking over 10 minutes and incurring high costs. How should you restructure the BigQuery table to reduce scan cost and query time for training?
options: Export the last 90 days of data to Cloud Storage as Parquet files before each training run using a Cloud Dataflow pipeline.
Create a BigQuery Materialized View over the last 90 days that refreshes daily and train on the materialized view.

Partition the table by the transaction_date column (DATE or TIMESTAMP partitioning). Training queries that include a WHERE transaction_date >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE(), INTERVAL 90 DAY) clause will prune partitions, scanning only the relevant ~10% of the table instead of 100%, dramatically reducing cost and query time.
Enable BigQuery BI Engine acceleration on the table to cache frequently queried rows in memory.
explanation: BigQuery partition pruning is the standard technique for limiting scan cost on time-series datasets. When a table is partitioned by a date/timestamp column and queries include a filter on that column, BigQuery scans only the matching partitions. For a 3-year table queried for 90 days, this reduces scanned bytes from 100% to ~8%, lowering cost (BigQuery charges per TB scanned) and query time proportionally. Optionally add CLUSTER BY product_id or customer_id to further optimize for secondary filters. Exporting to GCS adds pipeline complexity and extra storage cost. Materialized Views add refresh overhead and introduce potential staleness.

question: You are training a model to predict whether a bank customer will default on a loan next month, using 3 years of monthly customer transaction records. A junior data scientist proposes using scikit-learn's KFold with k=5, randomly shuffling all rows before splitting. Why is this approach problematic for this dataset, and what is the correct strategy?
options: KFold with k=5 is too few folds — increasing to k=10 provides more reliable cross-validation for financial data.

Random KFold introduces temporal data leakage: training folds will contain records from months AFTER the validation fold's records, meaning the model sees future data during training. This artificially inflates validation performance and causes the model to fail in production. Use TimeSeriesSplit: always train on earlier months and validate on later months, maintaining strict chronological ordering.
KFold is incorrect because the dataset is tabular — use Stratified KFold stratified on the default label to preserve class balance across folds.
Random KFold is acceptable here because each row represents a single customer-month snapshot — the temporal ordering of rows does not introduce leakage for tabular customer features.
explanation: Temporal leakage is a critical problem for time-series ML: when you randomly shuffle and split, records from month 30 appear in training folds while records from month 25 appear in the validation fold — the model learns from 'future' data when predicting 'past' outcomes. This produces misleadingly optimistic validation metrics. In production, only past data is available to predict the future — matching the correct evaluation protocol. TimeSeriesSplit (sklearn) implements expanding or sliding-window cross-validation where each fold's training data strictly precedes its validation data. For BigQuery-based pipelines: apply a WHERE transaction_date < cutoff_date filter for training and WHERE transaction_date >= cutoff_date for validation, then retrain on the full dataset before production deployment.

question: Your e-commerce training dataset includes a `product_id` column with 500,000 unique product IDs as a feature for purchase prediction. One-hot encoding would create a 500,000-dimensional sparse feature, making training computationally infeasible. Which encoding strategy is most appropriate for this high-cardinality categorical feature in a deep learning model?
options: Drop the product_id feature entirely — high-cardinality ID features are always too noisy to be useful for prediction.
Use target encoding: replace each product_id with the mean purchase rate for that product computed from the training set.
Use label encoding: assign each unique product_id a sequential integer from 1 to 500,000 and pass the integer directly to the model as a numerical feature.

Use a learned embedding layer: represent each product_id as a trainable dense vector (e.g., 64 dimensions) using `tf.keras.layers.Embedding(input_dim=500001, output_dim=64)`. Embedding weights are jointly optimized during training and capture latent product properties. For non-neural models, use feature hashing (sklearn's HashingVectorizer) to map IDs into a fixed-size hash space.
explanation: Embeddings are the standard approach for high-cardinality categorical features in deep learning: each category maps to a trainable dense vector optimized jointly with the rest of the model. This avoids the memory and sparsity issues of one-hot encoding. A common heuristic for embedding dimension is min(50, (cardinality+1)//2). In TFX, `tft.compute_and_apply_vocabulary` builds the vocabulary lookup; the integer IDs are then fed into an embedding layer. Target encoding is a strong alternative for tree-based models (XGBoost, GBT) but requires out-of-fold encoding to avoid target leakage. Label encoding assigns arbitrary integers that imply ordinal relationships — this is meaningless for nominal product IDs and produces poor representations without an embedding. Dropping a high-cardinality ID feature discards valuable product-level signal (purchase patterns, price range, category correlations).

question: You are training a binary image classifier to detect surface cracks in manufactured parts. Your dataset contains only 1,200 images total (900 training, 300 validation). The model consistently overfits: training accuracy is 98% but validation accuracy is 71%. You cannot collect more physical samples. Which data augmentation strategy most effectively increases effective training set diversity for this visual inspection task?
options: Duplicate all 900 training images and concatenate them — doubling the dataset to 1,800 images will reduce overfitting.

Apply random horizontal and vertical flips, random 90-degree rotations, and small random brightness and contrast adjustments. These transforms create visually distinct training examples without introducing semantically invalid images, since cracks can appear in any orientation and under varying lighting conditions. Implement in the training pipeline using `tf.keras.layers.RandomFlip`, `RandomRotation`, and `RandomBrightness`.
Apply extreme augmentations: randomly invert all pixel values, add 50% Gaussian noise, and randomly erase 40% of each image — more aggressive augmentation always improves generalization.
Generate 50,000 synthetic crack images using a GAN before training — GAN-generated images perfectly substitute for real data in small-dataset scenarios.
explanation: Data augmentation creates new training examples by applying label-preserving transforms. For visual inspection: (1) geometric transforms — horizontal/vertical flips and 90-degree rotations are valid because cracks can appear in any orientation; (2) color/lighting adjustments — brightness and contrast variation simulates different manufacturing lighting conditions. Invalid augmentations for this task: pixel value inversion changes the visual semantics; excessive noise masks the subtle crack texture the model must learn to detect; erasing large image patches may remove the defect itself, effectively mislabeling the sample. GANs require large training datasets themselves and typically introduce artifacts that harm downstream classifier accuracy. In Vertex AI Custom Training, augmentation layers should be placed inside the training pipeline only (not in the serving signature) by wrapping them in a `training=True` conditional, so augmentation runs only at training time.

question: You are preparing a tabular dataset for Vertex AI Custom Training. One numerical feature, `credit_utilization_ratio`, has 12% missing values. A colleague suggests dropping all rows where this feature is missing. What is the main problem with this approach, and what are better alternatives?
options: Dropping rows with missing values is best practice — missing values cannot be reliably imputed and always introduce model bias.
Replace all missing values with 0 — for numerical features, 0 is a safe neutral value that will not bias the model.

Dropping 12% of rows risks removing non-random records (missingness may correlate with the target — e.g., customers who report no credit utilization may have no credit history, which itself predicts default risk). Better alternatives: (1) median/mean imputation for MCAR data; (2) model-based imputation (IterativeImputer) when missingness correlates with other features; (3) add a binary indicator feature `credit_utilization_is_missing` to let the model learn the missingness pattern. Vertex AI AutoML Tabular handles missing values natively without preprocessing.
Use tf.Transform to encode missing values as -1 — gradient descent will learn to ignore the sentinel value automatically.
explanation: Missing data handling depends on the missingness mechanism: (1) MCAR (Missing Completely At Random) — mean/median imputation is safe; (2) MAR (Missing At Random, correlated with other observed features) — model-based imputation (sklearn's IterativeImputer) is more accurate; (3) MNAR (Missing Not At Random) — the fact of missingness is itself informative and a binary indicator feature should be added alongside imputed values. Dropping 12% of rows reduces training set size and introduces bias when missingness is correlated with the target. Vertex AI AutoML Tabular handles MCAR/MAR missing values natively using learned imputation strategies, so no preprocessing is required when using AutoML. For custom training, impute missing values inside tf.Transform so the same imputation logic runs at serving time — preventing training-serving skew.

question: You are preparing 500 GB of text data for a BERT fine-tuning job on Vertex AI Custom Training. The training script reads from Cloud Storage using `tf.data.Dataset`. Data is currently stored as CSV files. Each training step requires reading and parsing many small records, and GPU utilization is only 38% because the data pipeline cannot supply batches fast enough. Which data format change most effectively eliminates this bottleneck?
options: Convert CSV files to Parquet using Apache Spark on Cloud Dataproc — Parquet's columnar compression reduces file sizes and therefore read throughput from Cloud Storage.
Keep CSV but increase the number of parallel `tf.data.Dataset.map()` workers — more parallelism will overcome parsing overhead without changing the file format.

Convert to sharded TFRecord files (e.g., 200 files × 2.5 GB each): serialize examples as protocol buffers with `tf.train.Example`, write to GCS with `tf.io.TFRecordWriter`. Use `tf.data.TFRecordDataset` with `.interleave()` across shards, `.map()` with `num_parallel_calls=tf.data.AUTOTUNE`, and `.prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE)` so preprocessing overlaps with GPU compute. TFRecords eliminate CSV text parsing, enable efficient binary reads, and allow full pipelining of I/O and compute.
Store data in Cloud Bigtable and read rows directly in the training loop — Bigtable's low-latency key-value reads eliminate Cloud Storage I/O latency for ML training.
explanation: TFRecord is TensorFlow's native binary serialization format optimized for streaming large training datasets. Key advantages over CSV: (1) no text parsing overhead — each record is a pre-serialized binary proto; (2) sharding enables parallel reads across multiple files; (3) `tf.data` interleave across shards + prefetch creates a fully pipelined I/O→parse→GPU workflow where batches are ready before the GPU finishes the previous step — this is the primary mechanism for achieving high GPU utilization. Parquet is excellent for columnar analytical queries (BigQuery, Spark) but is not natively supported by `tf.data` and requires conversion at training time. Bigtable is optimized for transactional row lookups, not streaming sequential ML training reads. The correct workflow for Vertex AI Custom Training: preprocess to TFRecord once in a Vertex AI Pipeline step, then reference the GCS URI in training jobs.

question: Your team needs to preprocess 8 TB of raw clickstream logs stored in Cloud Storage using Apache Spark's GraphX library to compute user-level graph features (e.g., PageRank on user-product interaction graphs). The preprocessing runs as a one-time batch job with no streaming requirements. The team has strong Spark expertise and existing PySpark scripts. Should you use Cloud Dataflow or Cloud Dataproc?
options: Cloud Dataflow (Apache Beam): it is GCP's preferred managed data processing service and is always more cost-effective than Cloud Dataproc for any batch workload.

Cloud Dataproc (Apache Spark): Cloud Dataflow runs Apache Beam, which does not include Spark's GraphX library or MLlib. Dataproc runs native Spark clusters, supports the full Spark ecosystem (GraphX, MLlib, SparkSQL, PySpark), reuses the team's existing PySpark scripts without rewriting, and scales to 8 TB with autoscaling cluster configurations. For graph-based computations specifically requiring Spark APIs, Dataproc is the correct choice.
Cloud Dataflow with a custom DoFn that imports GraphX as a Python library — Apache Beam's runner can execute any Python library including Spark extensions.
Neither: use Vertex AI Pipelines with a Python component that loads the full 8 TB dataset into memory on a high-memory VM for processing.
explanation: Cloud Dataflow (Apache Beam) and Cloud Dataproc (Apache Spark) solve overlapping but distinct problems. Use Dataflow when: the team uses Apache Beam APIs, you need unified batch+streaming, or you want fully serverless auto-scaling with no cluster management. Use Dataproc when: existing code uses Spark-specific APIs (GraphX, MLlib, SparkSQL, RDD operations), Spark's ecosystem libraries are required, or migrating from on-premises Hadoop. GraphX is a Spark-specific graph computation library with no Beam equivalent — rewriting graph algorithms in Beam would be a significant engineering investment. Dataproc supports ephemeral clusters (create before job, delete after) that match Dataflow's cost model for batch jobs. For ML preprocessing pipelines, Dataproc jobs can be orchestrated from Vertex AI Pipelines using the `DataprocCreateClusterOperator` or the KFP Dataproc component.

question: Your tabular churn prediction dataset has 420 features, many of which are temporally derived metrics that are highly correlated (e.g., `avg_spend_7d`, `avg_spend_30d`, `avg_spend_90d`, `avg_spend_180d`). An XGBoost model trained on all 420 features overfits and training is slow. Which feature selection strategy is most principled for reducing dimensionality while preserving predictive power?
options: Apply PCA to reduce all 420 features to 50 principal components — PCA always improves model performance by removing noise dimensions.
Drop all features with any missing values, then randomly sample 50 features from the remaining set to ensure an unbiased reduced feature set.

Use a combination of correlation filtering and model-based importance: (1) compute a correlation matrix and for any pair with Pearson |r| > 0.95, drop the feature with lower variance; (2) train an initial XGBoost model on the reduced set and compute feature importances (gain) or SHAP values; (3) remove features with near-zero importance (e.g., bottom 10%). This data-driven approach retains features that genuinely drive predictions while eliminating redundancy without discarding useful information.
Always select features using domain knowledge alone — statistical methods introduce selection bias when applied to correlated time-series derived features.
explanation: For correlated tabular features in tree-based models, a two-stage approach is most principled: (1) Correlation filtering removes redundant features (keeping `avg_spend_90d` and `avg_spend_7d` when they are both highly correlated with `avg_spend_30d` adds no new information and inflates the feature space). Threshold |r| > 0.90–0.95 is common. (2) Importance-based pruning uses the model's own learned importance (gain, SHAP) to identify features that provide zero predictive value after correlation-based reduction. PCA removes interpretability by creating linear combinations of features — important for regulatory contexts like churn models where feature attribution is required. Random feature sampling discards high-value features arbitrarily. In Vertex AI pipelines, run this selection once on training data and apply the same selected features at serving time to prevent training-serving skew.
